does: This paper presents the Attention Redistribution Attack (ARA), a white-box adversarial technique that exploits internal attention mechanisms in safety-aligned language models. The core method identifies safety-critical attention heads and generates nonsemantic adversarial tokens that redirect attention probability mass away from safety-relevant positions using Gumbel-softmax optimization over the simplex. Evaluated on LLaMA-3-8B-Instruct, Mistral-7B-Instruct, and Gemma-2-9B-it against 200 HarmBench prompts, ARA achieves jailbreak rates of 30-36% using as few as 5 adversarial tokens and 500 optimization steps. The key mechanistic insight demonstrates a critical distinction: simply ablating top-ranked safety heads causes minimal failure (≤1 refusal flip), whereas redistributing attention through ARA tokens effectively bypasses alignment, suggesting safety relies on specific attention routing patterns rather than isolated head functions.
connects: This work extends a growing line of research examining internal mechanisms of LLM safety and alignment vulnerabilities. It builds on prior jailbreak methods that operate at semantic and output-logit levels, pushing investigation deeper into the mechanistic layer by targeting attention geometry. The paper connects to mechanistic interpretability research that investigates how transformer attention patterns implement model behaviors, alongside adversarial attack literature on neural networks. It relates to RLHF and instruction tuning safety research by examining what internal computations actually implement refuse behavior. The dissociation between ablation and redistribution findings advance understanding beyond prior safety mechanism studies that may have conflated head importance with functional necessity, responding to gaps in mechanistic understanding of how attention-based computation sustains alignment.
enables: This research opens several directions for both offense and defense. It enables future work on identifying and protecting safety-critical attention patterns through adversarial training specifically targeting attention redistribution. The mechanistic finding that attention routing patterns are central to safety implementation suggests novel defense mechanisms that harden attention computation rather than just training objectives. The low token count and optimization steps required point toward potential real-world adversarial concerns, motivating development of attention-based detection mechanisms. Methodologically, the approach of targeting softmax probability geometry could extend to analyzing other safety mechanisms or capabilities in transformers. Finally, it raises fundamental questions about the brittleness of RLHF-based safety and whether alignment requires deeper architectural or training-time changes to resist mechanistic attacks.
